<par>Benedict proposes a development from PLB <latinform>*lak-bai</latinform> > <latinform>*laŋ-wai</latinform> > <latinform>*mai</latinform> > <latinform>mɛ̄</latinform>. This analysis implies that there was a subsequent re-compounding of this <latinform>mɛ̄</latinform> with the morpheme <latinform>làʔ</latinform> 'hand'.</par>
<par>A more accurate gloss is 'avoid; evade; keep away from'.</par>
<par>STC tentatively assigns Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>tśhu</latinform> to <reconstruction>*tsyuk</reconstruction> (below, <xref ref="1356">#1356</xref>), though I prefer the present analysis.</par>
<par>The Lahu high-rising tone indicates that this was an originally stopped syllable with some kind of glottalized onset, perhaps <reconstruction>*ʔ-yak</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>Possible loan from Indic, cf. Sanskrit kapí.</par>
<par>The <latinform>-y-</latinform> medial in Lepcha derives from the PTB <latinform>*s-</latinform> prefix, see Benedict (1941).</par>
<par>This tone mark in STC is in error.</par>

<par>The <latinform>l-</latinform> prefix in this Tibetan form might well be a reduction of <xref ref="695">#695</xref>. Cf. pAN <latinform>*lima</latinform> 'hand; five'. (See Benedict 1975a:309.)</par>

<par>This is a bound form in Jingpho, occurring in the name of particular rattan species like <latinform>rīm-khá</latinform>, <latinform>rīm-šàt</latinform>, <latinform>rīm-šīn</latinform>. The free form for rattan is an open syllable, <latinform>rī</latinform> < <latinform>*rey</latinform>.</par>
<par>See Benedict 1940 #72. The semantic connection resides perhaps in the way branches may bend down like bows.</par>

<par>This WT form has been interpreted elsewhere as a fusion of <latinform>mi</latinform> NEGATIVE + <latinform>red-pa</latinform> BE, the latter related to WT <latinform>srid-pa</latinform> COPULA. See <plainlatinform>GSTC:64</plainlatinform>.</par>
<par>The second syllable of this WB form has a variant <latinform>bhyâ</latinform>.</par>
<par>Hakha Lai <latinform>θ</latinform> is from suffixal <reconstruction>*-t</reconstruction> (see STC pp. 102-3).</par>
<par>This root is in the indexes of STC (pp.201, 210), but is only mentioned in passing on p.33.</par>
<par>Benedict observes that Jingpho <latinform>ho</latinform> is a loan from Burmese and is usually pronounced <latinform>kho</latinform> (STC p. 33).</par>
